instructions.shared: Two non-negotiables for every DSA lesson, answer, quiz question, and recall cue: (1) every algorithm and data structure must include its time and space complexity with the reasoning that derives it from the mechanism — state the complexity after explaining how the algorithm works, not before; presenting Big O as an upfront label turns it into something to memorize rather than something to understand; (2) the goal is pattern recognition, not memorization — a learner who recognizes when a problem needs BFS but cannot recite it from memory is better prepared than one who has memorized BFS but cannot identify where it applies. Beyond these, the AI has full latitude over framing, narrative, pacing, and examples.
instructions.planning:
Use the dsa-depth-guide document for depth assignments. The prerequisite chain is a hard ordering constraint:

arrays + strings → linked lists + stacks + queues → recursion → trees + hash tables → graphs → sorting algorithms → dynamic programming

Recursion must appear before trees and graphs — it is not just a technique, it is the thinking model that makes recursive tree traversal and DFS tractable. Hash tables must appear before dynamic programming — memoization requires understanding O(1) key-value storage.

Graphs are non-negotiable and must not be treated as optional or advanced. Every software engineer encounters graph-shaped problems in real systems: package managers (npm, pip, cargo) use topological sort to resolve install order; build tools (webpack, make, gradle) use cycle detection on DAGs; network routing protocols (OSPF) use Dijkstra's algorithm; version control history (git commit graph) is a DAG; task scheduling with prerequisites is topological ordering; circular dependency detection in any module system is DFS cycle detection. Topological sort and cycle detection are the two graph algorithms closest to universal — no DSA course is complete without them. See dsa-graphs-core and dsa-graphs-advanced.

Two areas most courses under-teach: (1) problem-solving patterns — two pointers, sliding window, monotone stack, binary search on answer space — these patterns transfer across dozens of problems and are more valuable than knowing fifteen sorting algorithms; (2) the pre-code thinking step — reading constraints, inferring complexity budget from input size, choosing the data structure before choosing the algorithm.
instructions.lesson:
Minimum requirements per topic area — coverage floors, not structural templates:

Complexity: time and space complexity must be stated after the algorithm's mechanism is explained, not before. Giving Big O upfront makes it a label to memorize. Deriving it after the explanation makes it a conclusion the learner can own — 'there are log n levels of recursion and each level does O(n) work, so the total is O(n log n)'. Both time and space complexity must be covered. Stating just the result without the reasoning is insufficient.

Data structures: any lesson on a data structure must cover what problem it solves that simpler structures cannot solve efficiently. Do not teach the API without teaching the motivation.

Algorithms: every algorithm lesson must include the problem class it solves, its complexity with reasoning, at least one concrete real-world or system-level use case, and one situation where it is the wrong tool.

Graphs specifically: any graph lesson must make real-world grounding explicit. BFS and DFS are not academic exercises — they are how dependency graphs are resolved, file systems traversed, and social networks queried. The non-negotiable status of topological sort and cycle detection must be stated with concrete system examples.

Code: language-agnostic pseudocode or Python. When showing a core template (BFS queue loop, DFS recursion skeleton), show it cleanly once and reference it for related problems rather than rewriting it each time.
instructions.agent:
When asked which data structure to use: reason from the operations the problem requires. What is the dominant operation — lookup by key, ordered traversal, finding the minimum, LIFO access, shortest path between nodes? Each operation has a structure optimized for it. State the required operation, then name the structure and why.

When asked about complexity: walk through the reasoning, not just the answer. 'It is O(n log n) because each of the n elements is inserted into a heap, and each heap operation is O(log n)' is the expected form.

When asked about graphs: before suggesting an algorithm, establish whether the graph is directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted, and whether cycles are possible. These three properties determine which algorithm applies.

If a learner asks whether they really need to learn graphs: yes, unambiguously. Name three specific real systems that use graph algorithms — package managers, build systems, network routing — so the answer is grounded, not abstract.
instructions.quiz:
Test problem-solving process and complexity reasoning, not syntax or definitions. Do not ask: 'Write the code for merge sort from memory', 'What is a stack?', or 'Define Big O notation'.

Preferred question patterns:
- Given a problem description, ask which data structure fits and why the alternative is worse.
- Given an algorithm, ask for its time and space complexity with the reasoning.
- Given a scenario, ask which traversal to use and why (e.g., 'shortest path in an unweighted graph — BFS or DFS? Why can DFS not guarantee this?').
- Graph scenario: 'you are building a task runner — tasks declare dependencies on other tasks and the user may have introduced a cycle — which algorithm detects the cycle and which produces the valid execution order?'
- Pattern recognition: 'given a problem asking for the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of length k, which pattern applies and what is its complexity?'

Complexity analysis must appear in at least one question per topic. A learner who cannot reason about Big O has not learned DSA.
instructions.recall:
Recall cues should present a problem scenario and ask the learner to identify the structure, algorithm, or pattern that fits — not fill in a definition. The learner should name and justify the choice.

High-value recall targets: Big O of common operations on each structure, BFS vs DFS decision, which problems hash tables unlock, what makes topological sort applicable, the sliding window pattern trigger, the two-pointer pattern trigger, when DP applies vs greedy, and memoization vs tabulation trade-off. Specific scenario, difficulty, and framing are at the AI's discretion.

documents.title: Depth assignment guide for DSA topics
documents.content:
DEPTH ASSIGNMENT GUIDE — DSA Foundations

Critical (5–8 pages): full treatment, edge cases, failure modes.
- Big O analysis: deriving complexity, amortized analysis, space complexity, why constants drop
- Recursion as a thinking model: base cases, recursive trust, call stack, stack overflow
- Binary trees: all four traversals and their use cases, properties, height vs depth
- Hash tables: O(1) average ops, collision resolution, load factor, use cases
- BFS: queue template, shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-order, when BFS not DFS
- DFS: recursion/stack template, cycle detection, connected components, when DFS not BFS
- Topological sort: Kahn's algorithm, DFS-based sort, cycle detection on DAGs, real-world grounding
- Dynamic programming: recognizing overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure, defining state, memoization vs tabulation

Important (3–5 pages): explain the mechanism; recurring concept.
- Arrays and strings: contiguous memory, two-pointer pattern, sliding window pattern
- Binary search: O(log n), boundary pitfalls, search on answer space pattern
- Stacks and queues: LIFO/FIFO motivations, monotone stack pattern
- Linked lists: pointer manipulation, dummy node, fast/slow pointer (Floyd's cycle)
- BST: the sorted invariant, O(log n) balanced vs O(n) worst case
- Dijkstra's algorithm: weighted shortest path, min-heap, non-negative weight constraint
- Merge sort and quick sort: divide-and-conquer, O(n log n), when each is preferred

Medium (2–3 pages): concept + one or two examples.
- Heaps and priority queues: complete binary tree property, kth-largest/smallest problems
- Backtracking: recursion + undo, permutations/subsets/combinations template
- Union-Find: connected components, cycle detection in undirected graphs, path compression
- Greedy algorithms: local optimal → global optimal, when greedy is provably correct
- Minimum spanning tree: Kruskal's/Prim's, MST vs shortest path distinction

Light (1–2 pages): define and move on.
- Tries: prefix matching, autocomplete
- Bellman-Ford: negative weight edges, when Dijkstra's fails
- Floyd-Warshall: all-pairs shortest path (awareness only)
- Segment trees / Fenwick trees: range queries (awareness only)

documents.title: Big O complexity analysis — time and space
documents.content:
BIG O COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS

Big O describes how an algorithm's resource use scales as input size n grows. It captures the dominant term only — constants and lower-order terms are dropped because they become negligible at large n.

Time complexity: number of operations as a function of n.
Space complexity: additional memory used as a function of n.

Common complexities (fastest to slowest):
- O(1): constant — hash table lookup, array index access
- O(log n): each step halves the problem — binary search, balanced BST operations
- O(n): linear — single pass through array, BFS/DFS traversal
- O(n log n): sorting lower bound for comparison sorts — merge sort, heap sort
- O(n²): nested loops — bubble sort, brute-force pair problems
- O(2ⁿ): exponential — naive recursion for Fibonacci, power set generation
- O(n!): factorial — brute-force permutations

Deriving complexity: count how many times the dominant operation executes as n grows. A single loop over n elements = O(n). A loop inside a loop = O(n²) unless the inner loop shrinks (binary search inner step = O(log n) total). Recursion: draw the call tree, count nodes × work per node.

Amortized analysis: some operations are occasionally expensive but cheap on average. Dynamic array append is O(1) amortized — most appends are O(1), occasional resize is O(n), but the cost is spread across all n appends.

Why it matters in practice: an O(n²) algorithm on 10M records takes roughly 100T operations — hours to days. An O(n log n) algorithm takes ~230M operations — seconds. Reading input size constraints before writing code is how engineers know which complexity class is acceptable.

Must-know Big O for common structures:
- Array: O(1) access, O(n) search, O(n) insert/delete at arbitrary position
- Hash table: O(1) average lookup/insert/delete, O(n) worst case
- Binary heap: O(1) peek min/max, O(log n) insert/delete
- BST (balanced): O(log n) search/insert/delete
- Linked list: O(n) search, O(1) insert/delete at known node

documents.title: Arrays, strings, two pointers, and sliding window
documents.content:
ARRAYS AND STRINGS

Arrays: contiguous block of memory. O(1) random access by index. O(n) for search (unsorted), O(log n) with binary search (sorted). O(n) for insertion/deletion at arbitrary position because elements must shift.

Strings: immutable in most languages. String concatenation in a loop is O(n²) — each concatenation copies the entire string. Use a list/builder and join once.

TWO POINTER PATTERN
Use when: working on a sorted array or when two positions in the same array need to be tracked simultaneously. Reduces O(n²) brute-force pair problems to O(n).

Templates:
- Opposite ends (pair sum, palindrome check): left = 0, right = n-1, move toward center based on condition.
- Same direction (fast/slow, remove duplicates): slow tracks write position, fast scans ahead.

SLIDING WINDOW PATTERN
Use when: the problem asks about a contiguous subarray or substring satisfying a constraint. Avoids recomputing from scratch for each window.

Fixed-size window: maintain a running aggregate. Subtract the element leaving, add the element entering. O(n) instead of O(nk).

Variable-size window: expand right pointer when constraint is met, shrink left pointer when constraint is violated. Track the optimal window size seen so far.

PREFIX SUM
Use when: multiple range sum queries on a static array. Precompute prefix[i] = sum of elements from index 0 to i. Range sum [l, r] = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. O(1) per query after O(n) preprocessing.

When arrays are NOT the right structure: when you need O(1) lookup by non-integer key (use hash table), O(log n) ordered operations (use BST/sorted set), or O(1) insert/delete at the front (use deque/linked list).

documents.title: Linked lists, stacks, queues, and monotone stack
documents.content:
LINKED LISTS

A chain of nodes where each node holds a value and a pointer to the next node. O(n) search (no random access). O(1) insert/delete at a known node. O(n) insert/delete when the node must first be found.

Key techniques:
- Dummy head node: attach a fake node before the real head to simplify edge cases where the head itself is removed or reordered.
- Fast/slow pointers (Floyd's cycle detection): slow moves one step, fast moves two. If they meet, a cycle exists. Where they meet after resetting slow to head and advancing both one step identifies the cycle entry.
- Reverse in-place: track prev, curr, next; rewire curr.next = prev; advance all three.

STACKS
LIFO (Last In First Out). The call stack itself is a stack — function calls push frames, returns pop them.

Use cases: balanced parentheses validation, undo/redo, depth-first search (iterative), expression evaluation, backtracking.

Monotone stack pattern: maintain a stack where elements are always in monotonically increasing or decreasing order. Use for 'next greater element', 'largest rectangle in histogram', 'daily temperatures'. When a new element breaks the monotone property, pop and process until the property is restored.

QUEUES
FIFO (First In First Out). Core data structure for BFS traversal and task scheduling.

Deque (double-ended queue): supports O(1) append and pop from both ends. Use for sliding window maximum (monotone deque), BFS, and LRU cache implementation.

Key insight: stacks and queues are not just about storage order — they embody a processing discipline (depth-first vs breadth-first) that determines which problems they solve.

documents.title: Recursion as a thinking model and backtracking
documents.content:
RECURSION AS A THINKING MODEL

Recursion is not just a technique — it is the mental model that makes tree traversal, graph DFS, and dynamic programming natural. Teach it before trees and graphs.

Three parts of every recursive function:
1. Base case: the condition under which the function stops and returns directly. A missing or wrong base case causes infinite recursion and stack overflow.
2. Recursive case: reduce the problem to a smaller version of itself.
3. Trust the recursion: assume the recursive call correctly handles the smaller problem. Do not trace the entire call tree mentally — this is the mental model shift that unlocks recursion.

Common mistakes:
- Missing base case → stack overflow. The most common recursion error.
- Wrong base case (e.g., checking n == 100 when n can never reach 100) → infinite recursion.
- Not reducing problem size → infinite recursion.
- Off-by-one in base case → incorrect result on small inputs.

Call stack overhead: each recursive call uses stack frame memory. For deep recursion on large inputs (n > 10,000), iterative approaches or tail recursion optimization may be needed.

BACKTRACKING
Backtracking is recursion + an undo step. Explore a choice, recurse, then undo the choice before trying the next option. Use for: generating all permutations, subsets, combinations; N-Queens; Sudoku solver; path-finding with constraints.

Template:
  make choice
  recurse
  undo choice

Backtracking pruning: if a partial solution already violates a constraint, stop exploring that branch early (prune). Without pruning, backtracking can be O(n!) which is unusable for large inputs. With pruning, many problems become tractable.

When to use recursion vs iteration: recursion is natural when the problem has a self-similar structure (trees, graphs, divide-and-conquer). Iteration is preferred when the recursion depth is large or when you can maintain state explicitly with a stack.

documents.title: Binary trees, BST, heaps, and tree traversals
documents.content:
BINARY TREES

Each node has at most two children (left and right). Height = longest path from root to leaf. A balanced tree has height O(log n); a degenerate tree (all nodes in one direction) has height O(n).

Four traversals — what each is used for:
- Inorder (left → root → right): produces sorted output on a BST. Use for: sorted sequence, kth smallest in BST.
- Preorder (root → left → right): visits root first. Use for: copying/serializing a tree, prefix expressions.
- Postorder (left → right → root): visits root last. Use for: deleting a tree, evaluating expression trees, bottom-up computations.
- Level-order (BFS): visits level by level using a queue. Use for: tree height, minimum depth, zigzag traversal, connecting nodes at same level.

BINARY SEARCH TREE (BST)
Invariant: all nodes in left subtree < root < all nodes in right subtree. O(log n) for search, insert, delete in a balanced BST. O(n) worst case in a degenerate tree (equivalent to a linked list). Use a balanced BST (AVL, Red-Black) when balance guarantees are needed.

HEAPS AND PRIORITY QUEUES
A max-heap is a complete binary tree where every parent ≥ its children. A min-heap is the reverse. Implemented as an array (parent at i, children at 2i+1 and 2i+2).

Operations: O(1) peek min/max. O(log n) insert (bubble up). O(log n) delete min/max (sift down).

Use cases: kth largest/smallest element, merge k sorted lists, scheduling problems (always process the highest-priority task next), Dijkstra's algorithm.

Priority queue is the abstract interface; heap is the common implementation. Python: heapq (min-heap by default; negate values for max-heap). Java: PriorityQueue.

documents.title: Hash tables — the most practically important data structure
documents.content:
HASH TABLES

A hash table maps keys to values using a hash function that converts the key into an array index. O(1) average-case lookup, insert, and delete. O(n) worst case when all keys hash to the same bucket (hash collision).

Why O(1): the hash function computes the storage location directly — no traversal needed. Compare to: array (O(1) by index but keys must be integers), linked list (O(n) search), BST (O(log n) search).

Collision resolution:
- Chaining: each bucket holds a linked list of all keys that hash there. Simple to implement, degrades to O(n) when load factor is high.
- Open addressing: probe alternative slots on collision (linear probing, quadratic probing). Cache-friendlier than chaining.

Load factor: ratio of stored entries to total buckets. When load factor exceeds a threshold (~0.75), the table resizes (rehash all entries). Resizing is O(n) but happens rarely — amortized O(1) per insert.

When hash tables are the right structure:
- Frequency counting: count occurrences of each element in O(n).
- Deduplication: check membership in O(1) vs O(n) for unsorted arrays.
- Two-sum style problems: for each element x, check if target - x exists in O(1).
- Caching/memoization: store already-computed results keyed by input.
- Grouping: group elements by a computed property.

Critical insight: hash tables are how O(n²) brute-force problems become O(n). For any problem that involves 'have I seen this before?' or 'what is the count of X?', reach for a hash table first.

When hash tables are NOT the answer: when you need ordered iteration (use BST or sorted array), range queries (use segment tree), or guaranteed O(1) worst case (hash tables cannot guarantee this due to collisions).

documents.title: Graphs — BFS, DFS, and why they are non-negotiable
documents.content:
GRAPHS ARE NON-NEGOTIABLE

Every software engineer works with graph-shaped problems whether they recognize it or not:
- npm install: topological sort on a dependency DAG determines install order
- webpack/make/gradle: cycle detection on build dependency graphs
- Git commit history: a directed acyclic graph of commits
- GPS and network routing: shortest path on weighted graphs
- Social networks: friends-of-friends = BFS; community detection = connected components
- Task schedulers with prerequisites: topological ordering
- Deadlock detection in OS: cycle detection on resource allocation graph

A learner who reaches the end of a DSA course without BFS, DFS, topological sort, and cycle detection has gaps that will appear in real engineering work.

GRAPH VOCABULARY
- Vertex (node), edge (connection between nodes)
- Directed: edges have direction (A→B ≠ B→A). Undirected: edges go both ways.
- Weighted: edges have costs. Unweighted: all edges equal.
- Cycle: a path that returns to its starting node. Acyclic: no cycles.
- DAG: directed acyclic graph — the most common graph in real engineering systems.
- Connected component: a group of nodes where every node can reach every other node.

REPRESENTATIONS
- Adjacency list: graph[node] = [neighbor1, neighbor2, ...]. O(V+E) space. Best for sparse graphs (real-world default).
- Adjacency matrix: grid[i][j] = weight or 0/1. O(V²) space. Only for dense graphs or O(1) edge lookup.

BFS (Breadth-First Search)
Uses a queue. Explores level by level. Guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs.
Use for: minimum hops, nearest node, level-order tree traversal, flood fill.

Template:
  queue = deque([start]); visited = {start}
  while queue:
      node = queue.popleft()
      for neighbor in graph[node]:
          if neighbor not in visited:
              visited.add(neighbor); queue.append(neighbor)

DFS (Depth-First Search)
Uses a stack (or recursion). Explores as deep as possible before backtracking.
Use for: cycle detection, topological sort, connected components, path existence, all-paths problems.

Template (recursive):
  def dfs(node, visited):
      visited.add(node)
      for neighbor in graph[node]:
          if neighbor not in visited: dfs(neighbor, visited)

BFS vs DFS: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs; DFS does not. Use BFS for 'minimum X', DFS for 'does a path exist' or 'find all paths'. Both are O(V+E).

documents.title: Topological sort, cycle detection, Dijkstra's, and MST
documents.content:
TOPOLOGICAL SORT AND CYCLE DETECTION — NON-NEGOTIABLE

Topological sort: for a DAG, produces a linear ordering of nodes such that every directed edge u→v has u before v in the ordering. Applications: package install order (npm, pip, cargo), build system compile order (webpack, make, gradle), course prerequisite scheduling, CI/CD pipeline stage ordering.

Kahn's Algorithm (BFS-based):
  1. Compute in-degree (number of incoming edges) for each node.
  2. Queue all nodes with in-degree 0.
  3. Process each: decrement in-degree of neighbors. If in-degree reaches 0, add to queue.
  4. If output length < total nodes: a cycle exists.

DFS-based topological sort: run DFS, append each node to result after all its descendants are visited (post-order). Reverse the result. A back edge (visiting a node still in the current DFS path) = cycle.

Cycle detection:
- Directed graph: DFS with three states — unvisited, in-progress, done. An edge to an in-progress node is a back edge = cycle. This is how circular dependency errors are caught in package managers and build systems.
- Undirected graph: DFS tracking parent; if we visit an already-visited neighbor that is not the parent, cycle exists. Or use Union-Find.

DIJKSTRA'S ALGORITHM
Shortest path in a weighted graph with non-negative weights. Uses a min-heap (priority queue). O((V+E) log V).

Does NOT work with negative weight edges. Use Bellman-Ford (O(VE)) when negative weights are possible.

Real-world use: GPS navigation (OSPF routing protocol is a variant), game pathfinding (A* is Dijkstra's with a heuristic), network packet routing.

MINIMUM SPANNING TREE (MST)
Find the minimum total weight subset of edges that connects all nodes without cycles. Kruskal's: sort edges by weight, use Union-Find to add each edge if it doesn't form a cycle. Prim's: greedy, start from any node, always add the cheapest edge to the MST.

MST vs shortest path: MST minimizes total edge weight to connect all nodes. Dijkstra's minimizes path weight from one source to all other nodes. They solve different problems.

When MST applies: network design (minimum cable to connect all buildings), cluster analysis, approximate TSP solutions.

documents.title: Sorting algorithms and binary search
documents.content:
SORTING ALGORITHMS

Comparison sort lower bound: O(n log n). Any algorithm that determines order only by comparing pairs cannot do better in the general case.

Merge sort: divide array in half, recursively sort each half, merge. O(n log n) guaranteed — no worst case degradation. Stable (equal elements retain original order). Requires O(n) extra space. Use when: stability required, external sorting (data too large for RAM), linked list sorting.

Quick sort: pick a pivot, partition into elements < pivot and elements > pivot, recurse on both halves. O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst case (sorted input with bad pivot choice). O(log n) space (recursion stack). In-place. Fastest in practice for arrays due to cache locality. Use random pivot or median-of-three to avoid worst case.

Heap sort: build a max-heap, repeatedly extract max. O(n log n) guaranteed, O(1) space. Not stable. Cache-unfriendly — slower than quick sort in practice despite same complexity.

Bubble sort / Insertion sort: O(n²). Teach bubble sort only for intuition. Insertion sort has a practical advantage: O(n) on nearly-sorted data, used as the base case in hybrid sorting algorithms (Timsort, introsort).

Non-comparison sorts (O(n) when applicable):
- Counting sort: for small integer ranges [0, k]. O(n + k).
- Radix sort: sort digit by digit. O(nk) where k is number of digits.

BINARY SEARCH
Requires sorted input. O(log n). Halves the search space each iteration.

Off-by-one is the most common error: use left <= right for closed bounds. mid = left + (right - left) // 2 to avoid integer overflow.

Binary search on answer space: applies when the answer has a monotonic property — 'find the minimum speed such that all packages arrive on time'. Binary search on the possible speeds, not on an array. This pattern unlocks a large class of optimization problems that look nothing like 'find element in sorted array'.

documents.title: Dynamic programming — patterns, memoization, and tabulation
documents.content:
DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING

DP solves problems where the same subproblems are computed multiple times in a naive recursive solution. Store subproblem results to avoid redundant computation.

Two required properties:
1. Overlapping subproblems: the same subproblem appears in multiple branches of the recursion tree.
2. Optimal substructure: the optimal solution to the problem can be built from optimal solutions to subproblems.

If both hold, DP applies. If only optimal substructure holds but subproblems do not overlap, greedy or divide-and-conquer may be better.

THE HARDEST PART: defining the state. 'What is the minimum information needed to fully describe a subproblem?' That is the state. Once state is defined, the transition (how to combine subproblem results) usually follows.

Memoization (top-down): write the recursive solution, add a dictionary to cache results. Natural starting point — close to the brute-force recursive solution.

Tabulation (bottom-up): build the solution iteratively from smallest subproblems. No recursion overhead. Preferred when all subproblems must be solved and when recursion depth would cause stack overflow.

Classic patterns:
- 1D DP: Fibonacci, climbing stairs, house robber — each state depends on a few previous states.
- 2D DP: longest common subsequence, edit distance, 0/1 knapsack — state is (i, j) index into two sequences or (item, capacity).
- DP on intervals: matrix chain multiplication, burst balloons — state is [left, right] interval.
- DP on graphs/trees: max path sum in binary tree — combine results from children.

Memoization vs tabulation trade-off: memoization is lazy (only computes needed subproblems) and easier to reason about. Tabulation is faster (no recursion overhead, no hash map overhead) and avoids stack overflow for large inputs. Start with memoization, convert to tabulation if performance requires it.

documents.title: High-value problem-solving patterns
documents.content:
HIGH-VALUE PROBLEM-SOLVING PATTERNS

Patterns are the unit of transfer in DSA. Learning 10 patterns well prepares you for hundreds of problems; memorizing 100 specific solutions prepares you for only those 100.

Two Pointers: two index variables moving through an array. Use when: sorted array + pair condition (sum equals target), palindrome check, removing duplicates. Reduces O(n²) brute force to O(n).

Sliding Window: maintain a contiguous window that expands and contracts. Use when: 'maximum/minimum subarray/substring satisfying constraint'. Fixed size: update by subtracting leaving element and adding entering element. Variable size: expand right, shrink left when constraint violated.

Binary Search on Answer: when the answer has a monotonic property ('minimum X such that condition holds'), binary search over the answer space. The array being searched is the range of possible answers, not the input data. Applies to: minimum time to complete tasks, capacity planning, Koko eating bananas, split array largest sum.

Monotone Stack: maintain a stack where elements are always increasing (or decreasing). Pop when the monotone property is violated and process the popped element. Use for: next greater element, largest rectangle in histogram, daily temperatures, stock span problem.

BFS for Shortest Path on Implicit Graphs: when a problem involves minimum steps/moves between states, model the states as nodes and valid transitions as edges. Apply BFS. The graph is never explicitly built — states are generated on the fly. Use for: word ladder, sliding puzzle, minimum knight moves.

Union-Find: tracks which nodes belong to the same connected component. Two operations: find (which component is this node in?), union (merge two components). With path compression + union by rank: O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) per operation. Use for: connected components in undirected graphs, cycle detection, Kruskal's MST, grouping problems.

Backtracking: explore, recurse, undo. Use for: all permutations/subsets/combinations, constraint satisfaction, path problems with restrictions. Prune early when partial state already violates constraints.

documents.title: Common DSA misconceptions — flag and correct proactively
documents.content:
COMMON DSA MISCONCEPTIONS — FLAG AND CORRECT PROACTIVELY

1. 'Memorizing solutions is the same as learning DSA': interviews throw unseen problems. The skill being tested is pattern recognition across problem types — identifying that this new problem is a sliding window variant, not whether you have seen this exact problem before.

2. 'I can skip graphs — they're too advanced': graph problems appear in real systems that every engineer touches. npm dependency resolution, build tool ordering, network routing, and circular dependency detection all use graph algorithms. BFS, DFS, and topological sort are professional necessities.

3. 'Big O is just interview trivia': constants matter at small scale, but at 10M records an O(n²) algorithm takes billions of operations. Complexity analysis is how engineers decide which algorithm is viable before writing any code.

4. 'BFS and DFS solve the same problems': BFS guarantees the shortest path in an unweighted graph; DFS does not. Use BFS for 'minimum hops' problems. DFS is better for cycle detection, topological sort, and all-paths problems.

5. 'Hash maps are always O(1)': the worst case is O(n) when all keys collide to the same bucket. Average case is O(1) with a good hash function and a bounded load factor. Python dicts and Java HashMaps are amortized O(1), not guaranteed O(1).

6. 'Recursion is too slow, always use iteration': recursion has call-stack overhead but many problems are structurally recursive. Memoized recursion often matches iterative DP. The real concern is stack overflow for very deep recursion (n > 10,000), not performance in the average case.

7. 'Dynamic programming is just recursion with caching': the cache is an implementation detail. The intellectual work is recognizing overlapping subproblems and defining the right state. Two different state definitions for the same problem can lead to O(n) vs O(n²) solutions.

8. 'Binary search only applies to finding elements in sorted arrays': binary search applies to any monotonic function over any domain. Binary search on the answer space is a distinct and powerful pattern for optimization problems that look nothing like array search.

9. 'Greedy always works': greedy algorithms are only correct when a local optimal choice leads provably to a global optimum. This must be verified, not assumed. When in doubt, check whether DP is needed instead.
